subtopic_detail: Pertussis treatment in a young unvaccinated infant
stem: A 7-week-old infant presents with three weeks of progressive cough, now with paroxysms and post-tussive vomiting and one brief cyanotic episode. The infant has had no scheduled vaccines because the family delayed the 6-week visit. The mother had a non-productive cough for 4 weeks during the third trimester and did not receive an antenatal pertussis booster. The infant is feeding two-thirds of normal volumes and has no apnoeic pauses on monitoring.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 152, RR 44, T 37.2, SpO2 96% room air with brief desaturation to 88% during paroxysm
data_table.Examination: Mild subcostal recession, chest clear between paroxysms, anterior fontanelle soft, no rash
data_table.Investigations: Nasopharyngeal PCR positive for Bordetella pertussis; chest x-ray shows perihilar streakiness without focal consolidation; FBC WCC 28 with lymphocytosis 18

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate antimicrobial management?

options.text: Oral amoxicillin 30 mg/kg three times daily for 7 days as an outpatient with telephone review
options.rationale: Amoxicillin does not eradicate Bordetella pertussis from the nasopharynx and does not reduce transmission. (Source: eTG Antibiotic: pertussis)

options.text: Admit and start oral azithromycin 10 mg/kg once daily for 5 days plus household post-exposure prophylaxis
options.rationale: Infants under 6 months warrant admission for monitoring of apnoea and cyanosis; azithromycin reduces transmission and is the preferred macrolide under 1 month and remains first-line up to 6 months. (Source: eTG Antibiotic: pertussis)

options.text: Oral trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole 4 mg/kg twice daily for 14 days as an outpatient referral
options.rationale: Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole is a second-line option only when macrolides are contraindicated; it is also unsafe in infants under 2 months due to kernicterus risk. (Source: AMH Children's Dosing Companion)

options.text: Nebulised salbutamol 2.5 mg every 4 hours plus oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg for 3 days
options.rationale: Bronchodilators and corticosteroids have not demonstrated benefit in pertussis and may delay recognition of deterioration. (Source: RCH CPG: Pertussis)

options.text: Discharge with safety-netting only; antibiotics offer no benefit after 21 days of cough in any age group
options.rationale: Under 6 months, treatment is recommended up to 6 weeks after cough onset because of higher complication and transmission risk. (Source: RCH CPG: Pertussis)

explanation.summary: Pertussis in an unvaccinated infant under 6 months requires admission for apnoea and cyanosis monitoring and macrolide therapy to reduce transmission. Azithromycin is preferred under 1 month due to the pyloric stenosis signal with erythromycin and remains first-line through 6 months. Antibiotics shorten infectivity but do not abort the paroxysmal phase once established. Household contacts including the index parent should receive post-exposure prophylaxis with the same macrolide course.
key_points: Admit infants under 6 months with confirmed pertussis for cardiorespiratory monitoring
Azithromycin 10 mg/kg once daily for 5 days is the first-line macrolide for treatment and prophylaxis
Treat up to 6 weeks after cough onset in young infants and start contact prophylaxis simultaneously
explanation.pearls: Pertussis paroxysms peak at week 2-4 and persist for 6-10 weeks; once the cough is established antibiotics will not shorten symptoms but they will cut transmission by about 5 days.

subtopic_detail: Measles recognition in an unvaccinated traveller-returned child
stem: A 4-year-old returns to the emergency department two days after presenting with fever and cough. She returned 10 days ago from a 6-week visit to family in Indonesia and has no record of MMR vaccination. Today her fever persists with a new descending maculopapular rash starting on the forehead. There is no contact with strawberry tongue, parotitis, or known varicella.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 138, RR 26, T 39.4, SpO2 97% room air
data_table.Examination: Confluent erythematous maculopapular rash on face and upper trunk; bilateral conjunctival injection without discharge; white spots on buccal mucosa opposite the lower molars; pharynx mildly injected; chest clear; no joint swelling

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next public health action?

options.text: Discharge home with paracetamol, school exclusion for 5 days, and routine MMR catch-up at the general practitioner next week
options.rationale: Measles is a notifiable urgent disease and discharge home without isolation risks community transmission, including to vulnerable contacts. (Source: NSW Health: Measles Control Guideline)

options.text: Reassure parents the rash is roseola, advise antipyretics, and review at the general practitioner if persistent at 72 hours from rash onset
options.rationale: Roseola features defervescence preceding rash in toddlers under 3 and does not include conjunctivitis or buccal enanthem. (Source: RCH CPG: Rash - Viral Exanthems)

options.text: Admit for intravenous benzylpenicillin 60 mg/kg every 6 hours assuming early scarlet fever pending throat swab confirmation
options.rationale: Scarlet fever features a sandpaper-rough rash, strawberry tongue, and circumoral pallor; antibiotics are not warranted here. (Source: RCH CPG: Scarlet Fever)

options.text: Isolate in a negative-pressure room, notify the public health unit by phone today, and send measles IgM with throat and urine PCR
options.rationale: Koplik spots, fever, coryza, conjunctivitis, and travel exposure in an unvaccinated child meet a measles clinical case; airborne isolation and phone-notification within 24 hours are mandated. (Source: CDNA SoNG: Measles)

options.text: Order varicella PCR from a vesicular fluid swab and start oral aciclovir 20 mg/kg four times daily for 5 days empirically
options.rationale: Varicella presents with pruritic vesicles on an erythematous base in successive crops, not a confluent maculopapular descending rash. (Source: RCH CPG: Chickenpox)

explanation.summary: Measles is suspected in an unvaccinated returned traveller with the prodromal triad of cough, coryza, and conjunctivitis, plus pathognomonic Koplik spots and a descending rash. Phone notification of the local public health unit and airborne isolation are immediate priorities. Confirmatory testing is measles IgM serology together with throat and urine PCR. Post-exposure prophylaxis with MMR within 72 hours of exposure or NHIG within 6 days is offered to susceptible contacts identified by contact tracing.
key_points: Koplik spots plus fever, coryza, conjunctivitis, and descending rash define a clinical measles case
Negative-pressure isolation and same-day phone notification are mandatory
Confirm with measles IgM plus throat or nasopharyngeal and urine PCR
explanation.pearls: Measles infectivity spans 4 days before to 4 days after rash onset; contacts within that window need urgent susceptibility checks regardless of age.

stem: A 14-month-old is brought for review because the parents have noticed an inward turning of the right eye when she looks at distant objects, present consistently for the past month. There is no family history of amblyopia. She was born at term and otherwise tracks objects normally at close range. Photographs show the asymmetry from 8 months of age.

data_table.Examination: Right convergent strabismus with corneal light reflex displaced to the lateral limbus on the right; red reflex symmetric and present bilaterally; no leukocoria; cover test reveals refixation movement of the right eye; visual fields by confrontation grossly intact

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step?

options.text: Reassure that intermittent infantile strabismus typically resolves and review at the 18-month general practitioner check
options.rationale: Strabismus persisting beyond 4 months of age is abnormal and risks irreversible amblyopia without prompt assessment. (Source: RANZCO: Vision Screening Position Statement)

options.text: Prescribe occlusion patching of the left eye for 2 hours daily for 8 weeks then arrange optometry review
options.rationale: Patching is a treatment for amblyopia after orthoptic and refractive assessment, not a primary care first step. (Source: RCH CPG: Squint)

options.text: Refer urgently to a paediatric ophthalmologist within 4 weeks for cycloplegic refraction and orthoptic assessment
options.rationale: Constant unilateral strabismus persisting past 4 months requires paediatric ophthalmology assessment within 4 weeks to prevent amblyopia and exclude organic causes including retinoblastoma. (Source: RCH CPG: Squint)

options.text: Arrange urgent magnetic resonance imaging of the brain and orbits before specialist referral is considered
options.rationale: Neuroimaging is not first-line without focal neurology or other red flags; ophthalmology review prioritises refraction and red reflex assessment. (Source: RCH CPG: Squint)

options.text: Order a B-scan ultrasound of the right globe via the general practitioner pathway to exclude posterior segment pathology
options.rationale: Targeted imaging of the globe is determined by the ophthalmologist after clinical examination and is not a primary care ordering pathway. (Source: RANZCO: Vision Screening Position Statement)

explanation.summary: Persistent unilateral strabismus after 4 months of age is a referral indication to paediatric ophthalmology. The differential includes refractive error, anisometropia, and rarely retinoblastoma if the red reflex is abnormal or leukocoria is present. A symmetric red reflex on examination reduces but does not exclude retinoblastoma and prompt cycloplegic refraction and orthoptic measurements within 4 weeks are essential to start amblyopia therapy. Amblyopia rapidly becomes refractory after 7 years of age, so timely diagnosis matters.
key_points: Any constant strabismus past 4 months of age needs ophthalmology referral
Symmetric red reflex does not exclude retinoblastoma but lowers immediate concern
Cycloplegic refraction and orthoptic assessment within 4 weeks are the diagnostic basis
explanation.pearls: Pseudostrabismus from a wide nasal bridge mimics esotropia but the corneal light reflex remains central in both eyes; if displaced, refer.

stem: A 7-year-old is brought to the general practice with 2 weeks of scalp itch, and the mother has identified live head lice and eggs on combing. The child attends mainstream school and lives with two siblings without scalp itch. There is no recent overseas travel, no eczema, and the scalp is intact without bacterial superinfection.

data_table.Examination: Multiple eggs cemented to hair shafts within 1 cm of the scalp; two live lice identified on combing; no excoriation, no impetigo, no occipital lymphadenopathy

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate first-line management?

options.text: Wet combing with conditioner every 4 days for 2 weeks until no live lice are seen on three consecutive combings
options.rationale: Wet combing with conditioner is the first-line and most cost-effective approach in Australian primary care; it is also safe in pregnancy and infants. (Source: RACGP: Head Lice Management)

options.text: Topical permethrin 1% lotion applied to dry hair for 10 minutes then rinsed, repeated weekly for 6 weeks
options.rationale: Permethrin is an option but Australian resistance rates are substantial and prolonged 6-weekly repetition is excessive. (Source: AMH: Pediculosis)

options.text: Oral ivermectin 200 microgram per kilogram as a single dose with rinse-out repeat at 8 days
options.rationale: Oral ivermectin is reserved for treatment-refractory or institutional outbreaks, not as first-line in a routine case. (Source: AMH: Pediculosis)

options.text: Oral trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole 4 mg/kg twice daily for 10 days to clear lice and prevent impetiginisation
options.rationale: Antibiotics treat secondary bacterial superinfection only; they have no role in routine pediculosis without impetigo. (Source: eTG Antibiotic: skin and soft tissue)
source_refs: eTG Antibiotic: skin and soft tissue

options.text: Shave the head completely under general practitioner sedation and apply topical malathion 0.5% for 12 hours overnight
options.rationale: Head-shaving is not recommended and offers no benefit over evidence-based combing or topical therapies. (Source: RACGP: Head Lice Management)

explanation.summary: Pediculosis capitis in Australian primary care is managed first with wet combing using copious conditioner every 4 days until no live lice are detected on three consecutive sessions. Pyrethroid-based topicals are next-line given growing pyrethroid resistance. School exclusion is not required once treatment has started. Household members are checked but not treated empirically unless they harbour live lice.
key_points: Wet combing with conditioner is first-line and safe in any age
Treat only contacts with active live lice, not all household members empirically
School attendance is permitted once active treatment has commenced
explanation.pearls: Eggs more than 1 cm from the scalp are usually non-viable; presence alone does not equal active infestation.

sources.label: eTG Antibiotic: skin and soft tissue

stem: An 11-month-old presents with 18 hours of episodic inconsolable crying lasting 10 minutes every hour, drawing up his legs and now lying lethargic between episodes. Today there was one stool with red mucoid blood. He vomited twice with the second containing bile-stained material. He had a recent viral illness 5 days ago.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 168, RR 38, T 37.6, SpO2 98%, BP 86/52, capillary refill 3 seconds
data_table.Examination: Lethargic but rousable; right upper quadrant sausage-shaped mass palpable; bowel sounds reduced; perianal inspection shows redcurrant material on the nappy
data_table.Investigations: Point-of-care ultrasound by emergency consultant shows a target sign at the hepatic flexure with poor colour Doppler signal in the inner loop

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step?

options.text: Repeat clinical observation for 4 hours with oral rehydration before re-examining for ongoing concerns
options.rationale: Hemodynamic instability and the classic triad with mass make urgent intervention the priority; delay risks ischaemic perforation. (Source: RCH CPG: Intussusception)

options.text: Arrange an outpatient lower limb venous Doppler study and surgical clinic review the next morning before deciding on reduction
options.rationale: There is no clinical indication for vascular imaging; the reduced colour Doppler signal in the loop indicates compromised bowel perfusion. (Source: RCH CPG: Intussusception)

options.text: Proceed to laparoscopic exploration with manual reduction without further imaging or trial of pneumatic reduction
options.rationale: Surgery is indicated when pneumatic reduction fails or peritonitis or perforation is suspected; absence of those findings favours image-guided reduction first. (Source: RCH CPG: Intussusception)

options.text: Transfuse 10 mL/kg packed red cells over 30 minutes for ongoing redcurrant rectal bleeding before imaging review
options.rationale: Empiric transfusion is not warranted without haemoglobin documentation and ongoing significant blood loss; volume resuscitation precedes transfusion decisions. (Source: NBA: Paediatric Patient Blood Management)

options.text: Resuscitate with 20 mL/kg balanced crystalloid and proceed to fluoroscopic air enema reduction with surgical and theatre standby
options.rationale: Stabilise circulation then pneumatic reduction by an experienced radiologist with surgical backup is the standard of care for uncomplicated ileocolic intussusception without peritonitis. (Source: RCH CPG: Intussusception)

explanation.summary: Ileocolic intussusception in a toddler with the classic triad of episodic colicky pain, redcurrant stool, and palpable mass needs rapid resuscitation and pneumatic reduction with theatre standby. Ultrasound demonstrates a target sign with reduced internal colour Doppler when bowel viability is at risk. Image-guided air enema is successful in 70-90% of cases, with surgical reduction reserved for peritonitis, perforation, or failed enema. A pathological lead point should be considered in atypical age groups outside 3 months to 3 years.
key_points: Resuscitate with 20 mL/kg crystalloid before reduction
Air enema is first-line if no peritonitis or perforation
Surgical standby is essential because of perforation risk during pneumatic reduction
explanation.pearls: Recurrence after successful pneumatic reduction is about 10% in the first 24 hours; observe overnight before discharge.
